items.title: ‘This body, heavy with child, is drug dependent’
items.summary: By Olatunji Ololade From a black pot, one takes out white porridge. “A cikin bakar tukunya, a kan fitar da farin…
items.content_html:
By Olatunji Ololade

From a black pot, one takes out white porridge. “A cikin bakar tukunya, a kan fitar da farin tuwo.” Ask Ummi Ramatu. Patting her pregnant belly, she vows, “My child won’t be like me.”

There is a sullen accent to her pledge that sufficiently captures the inadequate sprawl of her life in Sanyaolu, Sabon Gari, Kano State.

 

Amid the squalor, Ramatu melds into the look of things: the houses, hunched like old hills in the dusty tract, and the flies circling her face in the heat, testing the damp shine of her skin. One lands boldly on her lower lip. Another tests the soft crescent of her eyelid. She flinches at first, jerking her head impatiently, swatting them off. But as the afternoon stretches and the heat intensifies, she stops resisting.

Leaning against the splintered wall of her booth, Ramatu’s back curved to the weight of the child shifting beneath her ribs. In her left hand, she holds a small, stained glass pipe. The stem is cloudy with residue, and its miniature bowl, charred to a dark sheen. She brings it to her lips with practised ease, inhales and holds her breath. When she exhales, her shoulders slacken. The flies persist, likewise the heat. But something inside her loosens its grip. Instantly, she unwinds with a knowing glance, as if she could see for the umpteenth time, the tarnished simplicities of her life.

“I will stop taking this thing,” she says. “It’s bad for the child.” A sliver of foreboding rasps through her lips. Sitting dishevelled in bed, with her hair tousled and speckled with mattress flakes, Ramatu draws from the glass pipe; eyes glazed over and both cheeks drawn inward, she sucked on it with conquering immersion, as if her next breath depended on each puff.

Exhaling, her eyes drift past the broken zinc roofs and dusty backstreets, into some place that exists only when the effect of the substance kicks in and begins to settle in her blood.

“I will not put to bed in this place,” she vows, even as customers steal into her bed, mostly grown men old enough to be her father. Then some young adults and teenagers, too. They are all driven by diverse kinks of the same fetish: to sleep with a young, heavily pregnant girl.

 It’s “good business.” She satisfies their cravings and gets paid. Ramatu, 20, is eight months pregnant. She is also a sex worker and substance dependent.

“I didn’t come here like this,” she states, bringing the pipe back to her lips. Six years ago, she fled to Kano from her birthplace, Tangaza, in Sokoto State after bandits laid siege on her village. “They were killing young and old men and abducting girls for ransom. The girls whose families couldn’t pay were turned into sex slaves,” she says. “I didn’t wish to end up as a bandit’s wife or sex slave.”

Thus, she fled with a neighbour’s daughter, an older girl who assured her of safety in Kano and employment in one of its thriving markets. Her first nights in the city, however, felt more like an initiation than escape.

Sabon Gari offered her no soft landing. Work proved elusive and shelter, conditional. Consequently, she was pressed “to squat in a brothel” and do menial jobs at food canteens around Abeokuta Street, close to Sabon Gari’s major hotels: Royal Park Hotel & Suites and Amiingo Hotels. It was in such liminal space that she got introduced to psychotropic substances.

“Just try it,” urged her roommate, handing her a stick of marijuana in the dim glow of their airless room. Thus, moved by curiosity and respect for the older girl, who sheltered her, Ramatu took her first puff on a Sunday night in 2021. Marijuana softened her worries, particularly the ache of displacement, and for the first time since arriving in Kano, Ramatu felt comforted.

Her journey into addiction wasn’t a single fall; it was a gradual erosion of will triggered by a first “harmless puff” and followed by a series of small yet grievous concessions. Soon, her “roommate and some of the other girls” urged her to try something stronger. And she found meth (methamphetamine).

“It was like stepping into an alternate reality,” she recalls. First, she “couldn’t sleep without it.” Then, she “couldn’t stay awake without it.” To sustain the habit, she needed money. So, she started with the “small hustle” – selling short-time sex, occasionally, after she closed from her day job as a restaurant salesgirl. This afforded her a decent meal and the ability to comfortably pay for her bed space. But she hungered for more.

“I didn’t plan to do sex work or take hard drugs,” she says. She simply wished to save up money to “start a wig store and rent a decent apartment.” But life happened. Ramatu’s introduction to “hard drugs” and sex work was subtle, almost benevolent. A joint passed between girls in a dimly lit room. Laughter that came too easily, and a light-headedness she never knew. The substances muted her miseries and made sex work less cringeworthy.

Her transition from marijuana to meth was unanticipated. It crept in, gradually, until it claimed her. By then, Ramatu had stopped counting the men and the money. Survival tapered into a peculiar rite: buy the meth, smoke the meth, repeat.

Before she realised what she had become, her transformation was complete. “I spend at least N80,000 weekly on meth, marijuana, and codeine,” she reveals.

Now, with a child growing inside her, it’s hard to quit. “I tried to stop. But when I don’t take it, I feel like my bones are breaking from inside,” she says, taking another deep puff.

Exhaling, she states, jocularly, “I thought I was going to become somebody.”

Ramatu’s fingers tremble slightly as she taps on the pipe. It was bare of the intoxicating crystal. She slumps against the wall and shuts her eyes, suggesting she has nothing else to say. The flies have settled now, their presence no longer an intrusion but a fixture, like the heat, the stench of sweat and Ramatu’s craving.

In that moment, she looks almost serene.

Not far from Ramatu, Endurance Daniel sits on a low stool at the entrance to her room, her hands folded over her pregnant belly. At 25, her demeanour morphs between resignation and a softness that has not entirely surrendered to the hardness of her circumstances.

“I’m from Cross River,” she says, “Ogoja by tribe.” Endurance relocated to Kano in 2022 in search of greener pastures. “I have been here four years now and my eyes have seen a lot…I don’t want any of my siblings or children to have a similar experience,” she states, her eyes scanning her surroundings.

 “Life here is really, really hard,” says the first child and only daughter of a family of four. “Before, I used to send money home every month. Even if it was small. But they haven’t heard from me in a long while. They don’t even know where I am.”

Having learnt hairdressing, she hoped it would sustain her in Kano, but she found herself contending with unemployment and homelessness. Frustrated, she sought a livelihood in commercial sex work. Her subsequent drift into substance dependence followed a familiar pattern. Long days and nights that yielded too little. Friends who offered escape in small doses, then in larger ones. At first, she binged just to forget and sleep without thinking too much. Soon, she became hooked.

Now seven months pregnant, Endurance no longer works the night as she used to. Customers throng her doorway but her body refuses the strain. She can only manage a few of them. The man responsible for her pregnancy offers some support, but it is insufficient against the demands of the third trimester.

“I’m full of regrets and everything. The kind of life I live here is not ideal. It destroys the future of we young ones. So, here I am. I am seven months pregnant. My parents have no idea where I am right now. I just need the government to assist us, to take us away from this very bad influence (substances). I need to get out of this place,” she pauses and adds resolutely, “I want to go back home.”

Ramatu and Endurance pose rare outliers in an otherwise common demographic. Being pregnant situates them within a small, largely unacknowledged network of young people who are substance dependent.

The crisis of substance abuse in Kano has long been framed as a hydra-headed problem: first, as the affliction of young men in gangs and political thugs. Then, as a challenge of females, mostly divorcees, widows and victims of spousal violence. Beneath that narrative subsists a more troubling reality: the involvement of women in Kano’s narcotics economy as both substance users and couriers.

According to the National Drug Law Enforcement Agency (NDLEA) 2024–2025 statistics, of over 1,345 suspects arrested in 2024, forty-four were women. In 2025, 43 women were among the 1,389 arrested. The numbers appear small, considering the burden of stigma in a society where a pregnant, substance-dependent sex worker is publicly condemned and patronised in secret. The double standards exacerbate the crisis, particularly in a hot spot like Sabon Gari, where the lust for a pregnant sex worker flourishes as a kink and hidden subculture.

 

The cost of escape

Unlike Ramatu and Endurance, Halima isn’t pregnant. But with them, she shares a hankering for codeine, marijuana and methamphetamine (aka meth or ice). Oftentimes, she consumes the triad of substances, spending as much as N100,000 weekly to sustain the habit.

“Without drugs,” she says, “I feel lost.” Originally from Damagaram in the Republic of Niger, 25-year-old Halima also has ties to Katsina State. Five years ago, she relocated to Kano, but an interplay of “daily miseries and peer pressure” led her into substance abuse.

“I experience intense distress and a constant feeling of despair if I don’t take hard drugs. I am deeply concerned about the direction my life has taken,” laments Halima. She has “a Diploma in Accounting and Auditing.” She also needs a decent job and hopes to get married, if she could quit soon.

Zainab’s dependence is even more severe. “I spend over N170,000 weekly, on crack cocaine and codeine. I take them regularly to feel functional,” she reveals.

Even though she dreads the consequences, she is unable to control her craving. “It feels like I am being driven by a force beyond my will. Nearly all the money I make is squandered on hard drugs. I feel overwhelmed,” she laments.

Hassanatu has, however, turned dependence into a means of survival. Sitting outside her stall, off Fagge Road, the 22-year-old highlights her role as a middleman. “I don’t sell drugs because I like it,” she says. “I sell it to sustain my habit. If I don’t sell, I cannot afford it.”

As a retailer, Hassanatu serves as an indispensable fragment of Kano’s drug trafficking network. “Times are hard,” she states, stressing that when business (commercial sex work) is at its lowest ebb, she sells substances to survive.

“I help people, especially those coming from outside Sabon Gari, to buy drugs. Many come from as far as BUK (Bayero University, Kano). Because the NDLEA frequents those areas, most student users don’t wish to be caught or seen patronising drug dealers around the school. Here, I help them get what they need.”

 

The Kano conundrum

In Kano, arguably Nigeria’s most populous state and industrial hubs, the crisis of substance dependence has assumed worrisome dimensions.

In August 2021, the NDLEA disclosed that Kano had roughly two million drug users—about 16 per cent of its population—with the agency’s chairman, Buba Marwa, noting that “one in every six residents is addicted.”

As of early 2025–2026, NDLEA and UNODC corroborate that Kano has the highest rate of substance abusers aged 15 to 64, with an estimated 1.07 million users, making it the epicentre of substance abuse in the Nigeria’s North West.

Between 2018 and 2019, nearly 15% of Nigeria’s adult population (around 14.3 million people) reported a “considerable level” of use of psychotropic drug substances, a rate much higher than the 2016 global average of 5.6% among adults, according to a survey led by the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) and the Centre for Research and Information on Substance Abuse (CRISA) with technical support from the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) and funding from the European Union.

It showed that the highest levels of drug use were recorded among people aged between 25 and 39, with cannabis being the most widely used drug. Sedatives, heroin, cocaine and the non-medical use of prescription opioids were also noted. The survey excluded the use of tobacco and alcohol.

It also excluded users like Ramatu, Endurance, Halima, and a host of others, who substance dependent. Kano, their base and epicentre of the crisis, poses a major concern in the North-West, where UNODC estimates substance abuse prevalence at 12 per cent—about three million users annually. Of this figure, Kano alone accounts for over one million users across the region’s seven states.

Kwara and Gombe lead the North-central and North-east with 13 and 21 per cent prevalence, respectively, while Delta (18 per cent), Imo (18.1 per cent), Lagos (20 per cent), and Oyo (15.3 per cent) record high usage rates across the southern regions.

 

Consequences of substance dependence

More unsettling is the magnitude of the crisis and the capacity to respond to it. At the Aminu Kano Teaching Hospital (AKTH), one of the most prominent referral centres in the region, the de-addiction unit admitted just 166 patients over a three-year period between 2018 and 2020. That translates to an average of about 55 or 56 admissions a year. The majority of those admitted are young, between the ages of 16 and 35, many of them arriving at a point when dependence has already taken hold

Set against Kano’s wider reality, the numbers become even more worrisome. The gap between those who need help and those who receive it, is highlighted by the findings of the AKTH study. The research, carried out by a team led by Prof. Auwalu Sani Salihu, asserts that at such admission rates, it would take decades to reach even a fraction of those affected, assuming no new cases emerge within the period.

Prof. Salihu, who is currently the Medical Director of the Federal Neuro-Psychiatric Hospital, Dawanau, Kano State, notes that, “Substance abuse in Kano is closely linked to youth unemployment, poverty, rapid urbanisation, overcrowded living conditions, family disintegration, and lack of recreational and economic opportunities.”

While many young people indulge as a coping mechanism, he states that, “it is often an entry point into cycles of crime, ill health, and social exclusion.” According to him, substance use disorder (SUD) is best understood “as a chronic, relapsing but treatable brain disorder, rather than a moral failure or lack of willpower.” Repeated exposure to psychoactive substances, he explains, produces measurable changes in brain circuits responsible for reward, motivation, impulse control, and decision-making.

This reduces an individual’s ability to quit despite harmful consequences. Like other chronic medical conditions such as hypertension or diabetes, he says, “SUD require early diagnosis, evidence-based treatment, long-term follow-up, and relapse prevention, not punishment or social exclusion.”

 The inability to quit has grown into a major crisis. The consequences are highlighted in the growth and spread of violence across the state.

A 2026 confidential report compiled by the Kano State Police Command and cited by the State Commissioner of Police, CP Ibrahim Adamu Bakori, links up to 70 per cent of major crimes in the state to substance abuse. Police records from 2025 show 3,081 arrests for serious offences, with authorities acknowledging that a significant proportion of suspects were under the influence of narcotics. The proliferation of informal “drug joints” has also altered entire neighbourhoods, breeding fear and distrust among residents, notes a senior police officer, pleading anonymity.

The health consequences, though less visible than violence, are no less devastating. Clinical observations from treatment centres and research findings in Kano consistently indicate a surge, about 92 per cent, in drug-induced psychiatric disorders, including psychosis and severe depression, as the leading causes of psychiatric hospitalisations in the state. And approximately 65.6% of drug-related incidents in Kano result in death, according to a subsequent study.

Efforts at treatment remain limited in a state with a population approaching 20 million, and with only a handful of specialist psychiatrists and functional rehabilitation centres. There are limited specialised treatment facilities aside from the Federal Neuropsychiatric Hospital, Dawanau, Kano and the Aminu Kano Teaching Hospital, Kano, which are overstretched, and where access is often constrained by responsibilities that treatment programmes are ill-equipped to accommodate.

Zainab, a single mother and trader in Naibawa, frames the dilemma plainly. “If I go in for treatment, who will take care of my child?”  On the flipside, some parents of substance-dependent youths would rather take their wards to traditional or religious-themed rehabilitation centres. Because, “They are cheaper to consult. And you’d rest assured that your child is getting both spiritual and psychological help,” says Mahmud Mohammed, a clothes dealer.

 

The male face of dependence…

While this narrative turns its gaze at women, The Nation’s encounter with some teenage boys and young men driving Kano’s drug economy as users, enforcers, errand boys, couriers, and pimps, is noteworthy.

 To understand the women, one must first understand a young man like Nurairai, who orbits and exploits them, even while battling his own craving.

From Sanyaolu through Fagge, Nurairai shuttles back and forth like a cocky prince of the barrio. He is at once a peddler and a broker of sexual and psychotropic pleasure. The 25-year-old blurs the line between narcotics and sex merchandising as he pimps both substances and sex workers. He is also dependent.

“When I cannot access drugs, I get very agitated and become aggressive, to the point of harming someone just to satisfy my urge,” he reveals. “This is not the life I want for myself. I recognise the damage it is causing to me and those around me. I sincerely desire a way out of all this.”

Nurairai believes he can overcome his dependence if he can secure “access to a reasonable business fund and the opportunity to engage in a stable, productive livelihood.”

And in Kundila, far from Nuraira’s base in Sabon Gari, Fa’iz Bashir, 18, recalls his past filled with regrets. “My journey into substance abuse began under circumstances I deeply regret,” he says. “During a youth horse racing event called ‘Iklisa,’ I was involved in an accident that led to someone being seriously injured. That unfortunate incident resulted in my imprisonment for one year. While in prison, I was exposed to armed robbers, kidnappers, and drug traffickers. It was within that environment that I was introduced to drugs.”

Today, Bashir grapples with the consequences of that exposure. “I have been struggling with drug use for the past two years, and I can honestly say that it has brought me no peace or fulfilment. There are days I wake up without any money, yet I find myself craving substances so badly that I easily waste as much as N50,000 on drugs,” he says.

Despite all that has happened, Bashir is determined to change. “I have completed my secondary education, and I currently buy and sell mobile phones. I desire a better life. I want to return to the person I used to be,” he says.

 Then, there is Najeeb Mohammed, a prominent feature of Kundila Housing Estate. “I’m addicted to hard drugs,” he says, without ornament, as though naming it so plainly might strip him of dependence. There is no defiance in his voice, only a tired transparence.

“I think I can’t live a sweet life without drugs. I’m addicted to CK (crack cocaine),” he says, revealing that he “started using hard drugs 15 years ago, at age 19.” He is not happy to use CK “because it is a very, very bad drug,” he laments, adding that he also takes rohypnol and codeine.

Mohammed, 34, “studied Mathematics and proceeded to Ghana to earn a postgraduate degree in International Relations and Diplomacy.” But somewhere along the way, he lost his path.

“I was 19 when my friend introduced me to hard drugs. He’s a sort of millionaire in my state. Because I didn’t have money, I was captivated by everything that he did with money,” says Mohammed, stressing that his exposure to wealth made indulgence seem harmless. He enjoyed the access it granted and the absence of immediate consequences. Soon, his curiosity sidled to experimentation and, then, dependence.

Mohammed claims he started taking cocaine because he couldn’t find a job. Work remains out of reach, and occasionally coming in cynical forms. The big politicians, he says, use men like him only as political thugs. “They only use and dump us…That’s life,” he states, with exhaustion. “You can’t escape your destiny.”

Mohammed’s verdict hangs heavily, not because it is true, but because so many have come to believe it.

 

The economy of addiction

There is no gainsaying that Kano’s narcotics scene has expanded. For decades, cannabis dominated its streets, cheap and widely available. Today, it shares space with a more precarious mix of substances that are cheaper and far more destabilising in consequence.

Crack cocaine is widely accessible even as heroin gradually creeps into the urban landscape. Methamphetamine, known locally as “ice,” is particularly prized for its intensity and speed at triggering dependence. Alongside these are pharmaceuticals, codeine syrup and tramadol, which are often diverted from legitimate supply chains into Kano’s informal markets.

On the streets of Sabon Gari, for instance, a diverse cocktail of substances is circulated and consumed within an informal economy. Even when prices fluctuate, the structure holds. A bottle of codeine sells for between N5,000 and N8,000. Tramadol tablets can be obtained for as little as N50 depending on the brand. Cannabis is negotiated and sold in wraps of N500 – N1,000, its cost determined by whether it is laced with crushed meth or opioids. And while meth commands a premium, at N3,000 per ounce, the demand for it continues to rise.

For the dependent, daily spending can range from N3,000 to N20,000. To sustain the habit, many engage in petty trading, commercial sex work, theft, and substance sales.

 

Limits of seizure

To curb the malady, the state government and the NDLEA stage periodic crackdowns on substance warehouses and points of sale. As of May 4, 2026, the NDLEA Kano State Command has raided major drug hubs, uncovering underground drug storage sites comprising caves and tunnel systems in Rimin Kebe. The discovery signals a shift from scattered peddling to covert, networked trafficking infrastructures. It also raided the Sani Abacha Stadium area in Kofar Mata, Fagge, Kasuwar Rimi, Medile, Badawa, Sabon Gari, in Tashar Rami.

At the Bayero University Kano (BUK)’s old site area, six suspects were arrested, following a report by the school management about the sale and consumption of substances in the area, according to the Public Relations Officer of the NDLEA Kano Strategic Command, Assistant Superintendent of Narcotics, Sadiq Maigatari.

During previous operations between January and March 2026, the NDLEA intercepted over 2.1 tonnes of illicit drugs, including 75,000 Pregabalin capsules, codeine, and cannabis, and arrested 352 suspects.

On April 11, 2026, operatives conducted coordinated raids across Filin Idi, Hotoro, Mariri, Fagge, and Farm Centre, arresting 21 suspects and seizing a range of substances.

These follow seizures made in previous years. For instance, operatives intercepted more than five million tramadol tablets in a single operation in January 2024, in Bacharawa and Gadar Tamburawa areas.

While the operations seemed decisive, their impact was temporary. Within days, activity resumed at the raided hubs, as new dealers emerged to replace those arrested. In 2025 alone, 1,389 arrests were made. In 2024, 128 convictions were secured, while in early 2026, 352 arrests were made.

And in a major push to combat rising drug abuse, the Kano State Police Command recently implemented an anonymous, digital-driven reporting system to encourage residents to reveal substance dealers and users.

The initiative, championed by the Command’s Public Relations Officer, CSP Abdullahi Haruna Kiyawa, leveraged social media to allow residents to identify culprits without risking their personal safety.

Within just 24 hours of the initial public call for information, community members provided names and locations that helped the police identify over 2,000 suspected substance dealers and users. Rapid raids across high-risk areas like Dala, Rimin Kebe, Jakara, and Mandawari resulted in hundreds of arrests.

This was in direct response to data provided by the State Commissioner of Police, CP Ibrahim Adamu Bakori, who noted that roughly 70 percent of crimes in Kano is linked to substance abuse.

Still, supply remains steady, and demand continues to grow.

And this has been blamed on the nature of enforcement itself. Raids, argues security expert, Francis Ikomu, tend to target what is evident and immediate about the problem. “That is, the street-level actors whose arrest and removal hardly affect the larger system. Those higher up the chain, with greater resources and connections, remain largely untouched,” he says.

 

Following the supply chain

To understand why law enforcement has failed to curb the flow, it is necessary to look beyond the observable economy of street dealers and into the networks that sustain it. That operatives intercepted more than five million tramadol tablets in a single operation in January 2024, along the Zaria–Kano Road, in Bacharawa and Gadar Tamburawa areas, and another 5.6 million tablets of tramadol in April 2024, points to organised distribution systems operating across state lines, and embedded in commercial routes predating the crisis.

Also, in 2024, Salisu Mohammed, a dismissed police inspector with multiple prior convictions for cocaine trafficking, was sentenced to five years in prison. His case highlighted how deeply compromised the system can become when supposed law enforcers serve as substance dealers.

The figures arrested in raids are, as one NDLEA officer puts it, “the smallest part.” Above them are layers of importers, distributors, and facilitators who operate with greater insulation and, often, greater protection. They are harder to reach and rarely seen.

At its outermost edge, the supply chain begins beyond Kano’s horizons. Narcotics and controlled pharmaceuticals slip into Nigeria through porous land borders and formal entry points alike, including the Mallam Aminu Kano International Airport.

Whether concealed in spare parts consignments, packaged as innocuous household goods and food items, or hidden in cross-border trade, traffickers exploit both formal logistics systems and informal trade routes, turning Kano into a northern gateway for narcotics flowing across West Africa.

Once inside the state, the drugs do not scatter randomly; large-scale distribution gravitates toward commercial drug markets such as the Kanawa Pharmaceutical Coordinated Wholesale Centre, alongside older, loosely regulated trading clusters.

 From these hubs, the supply chain distils into a web of micro-distribution networks. In neighbourhoods such as Rimin Kebe, Badawa, Fagge, Sabon Gari, and Kofar Mata, drugs are stored, repackaged, and moved in smaller consignments. Motor parks, particularly in transit corridors like Tashar Rami, serve as logistical channels, where substances are ferried across districts via routine transportation, hidden in false compartments of vehicles, dissolved into or embedded within everyday items, including black soap, food containers, and textiles.

Recall that NDLEA operatives uncovered, earlier this month, underground caves and tunnel systems in Rimin Kebe. They were engineered specifically for the storage and protection of illicit substances, thus revealing a shift from opportunistic trafficking to infrastructure-based criminal enterprise.

Kano faces a distinct crisis of pharmaceutical opioids and sedatives: tramadol, codeine syrup, diazepam, Rohypnol, and Pregabalin, of which many are smuggled or diverted from legitimate supply chains into the palms of Kano’s substance-dependent youths.

Among younger users, inhalants and locally concocted mixtures like “suck and die” are readily accessible. This is particularly worrisome as it involves neither sophisticated importation nor capital-intensive networks.

Yet, despite ongoing interventions, substance distribution in Kano remains a cat-and-mouse scuffle. While each raid displaces the trade and reshapes its routes, the supply chain simply adapts but rarely breaks.

Thus, by the time a tablet of tramadol is swallowed in a dimly lit alley in Fagge, or a bottle of codeine syrup passes between trembling hands in Sabon Gari, it has already travelled a long, deliberate path, across borders and local markets.

 

To tame a scourge

One critical weakness in Kano’s current response is its fragmented nature. The responsibilities for drug control, prevention, treatment, rehabilitation, and social reintegration are spread across multiple ministries, departments, and agencies, often with overlapping mandates but limited coordination, argues Prof Salihu.

 To combat this, he recommends the establishment of a Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Agency (SAMHSA) as was done recently in the neighbouring Kaduna State. This, he explains, would provide a central coordinating body to address the challenge of coordinating prevention, treatment, and rehabilitation services across sectors.

It would also “Develop and enforce minimum standards and quality assurance mechanisms for treatment centres, support training and capacity building of healthcare and social service workers,” he says.

But that is in the long run; in the short run, the Governor Abba Yussuf-led administration must work with other stakeholders to reclaim disillusioned and dependent youths like Mohammed from the grip of psychotropic substances.

Mohammed’s story transcends a dreadful dependence. It is marked, time and again, by his attempt to stop. He has quit for months, and, once, for a full year. Still, he relapsed.

There is a rhythm to how he smokes crack cocaine now. During Ramadan, he stops and attempts to pray away his dependence. Then the cycle resumes, often around festive seasons, when money and substances flow more freely. “Now, I use drugs from Salah to Salah,” he says, as if measuring his struggle against the sacred markers of the Islamic calendar.

“I don’t want to end my life like this,” he says, adding that he wishes to get married by December. “No matter what, I intend to marry. So, I have to stop doing drugs.”

While Mohammed embodies Kano’s substance scourge, in the travails of Endurance and Ramatu, the crisis finds its most human expression.

A lot has been written about Kano’s substance-dependent females, mostly divorcees or wives stuck in loveless marriages. The subjects here, however, personify a different demographic: unmarried, pregnant, young, sex workers, who are also substance dependent, among others.

 “Many of them have been traumatised by their circumstances, often from childhood. They live with the scars as we speak, and their only recourse to dealing with their pain is substance abuse, which never benefits them or anyone. They need help fast,” says consultant clinical psychiatrist and aid worker, Rodiya Oliyide.

If you think that education offers some form of protection. Ask Mohammed. If you think life as an unmarried, pregnant, substance-dependent sex worker is a walk in the park. Ask Ramatu. If you think quitting the habit should be easy, ask Zainab.

Together, their lives mirror the same fault lines and the struggles of countless others experiencing the same cycles, often unseen and unheard, trapped between the desire to stop and the absence of a system strong enough to support them.

Yet, their story does not end here. It lingers instead, unresolved, like their battles with substances. Somewhere in the undertow grows Endurance and Ramatu’s emergent fetuses, unsuspecting yet imperilled by the prospective mothers’ meth dependence.

Fadekemi Oguntoyinbo, a paediatrician, notes that pregnant women who ingest psychotropic substances, especially those containing cocaine or meth, risk causing severe health problems for their babies. According to her, substances taken during pregnancy may cause the blood vessels that carry blood to the uterus and placenta to constrict. Thus, less oxygen and fewer nutrients reach the fetus.

A pregnant woman using substances risks a miscarriage and stillbirth. And if the child survives, it may suffer defects of the brain and spinal cord, urinary tract and bone defects, she explains.

The womb is supposed to be a developing child’s safe space. But Endurance and Ramatu feed toxic nutriment through its wall: methamphetamine, tramadol, rohypnol, cannabis, and codeine; then occasionally, crack cocaine.

The substances impact a growing fetus the same way a loaded rifle spits bullets at a malnourished child. But some mothers are past caring.
